topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara sat at her desk, the glow of her laptop illuminating the stark white page. She had been searching for a specific piece of advice, a template for crafting a compelling personal statement, when she stumbled upon it. It was titled, "The Quiet Observer," and the prose was so polished, so perfectly articulated, it felt alien. Her fingers trembled as she scrolled, reading the narrative about a childhood moment, the introspection so deep it felt invasive. A coldness washed over her, settling deep in her chest. She reread a sentence, her eyes darting back and forth, searching for a flaw, a sign that this wasn't real. When she finally closed the document, the sound of the click echoed loudly in the silent room. She stood up abruptly, pacing the small space, her breath coming in shallow, quick gasps.

[story 2]

Mark found the file tucked away in a forgotten folder on his cloud drive. It was his own essay, submitted months ago, now appearing in a shared repository labeled 'Successful Submissions.' His stomach clenched, a tight, sickening knot forming just beneath his ribs. He leaned closer to the screen, his knuckles whitening as he gripped the edge of the desk. The words, his own carefully chosen reflections on overcoming a challenge, were laid bare, scrutinized by unseen eyes. A wave of intense unease swept over him, making his vision momentarily tunnel. He backed away slowly, his chair scraping against the floor, the sound grating and unwelcome. He couldn't bring himself to look at the screen again; the sheer exposure felt like a physical assault.

[story 3]

When Chloe opened the shared drive, she nearly dropped her coffee mug. There, nestled between several other polished submissions, was her essay. It was hers, every carefully worded sentence, every vulnerable admission, displayed in pristine format. A sudden, sharp intake of breath hitched in her throat. Her hands flew up, covering her mouth, muffling a sound that was more of a choked squeak than speech. Her heart hammered against her ribs, a frantic drumbeat against the sudden stillness of the room. She stared at the text, her gaze unfocused, as if trying to pull the words back into the ether. Slowly, deliberately, she minimized the window, her movements stiff and unnatural, and walked to the window, needing the unfiltered expanse of the outside world.
